items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What is opportunity cost?
items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNP?
items.correct: GDP measures output produced within a country's borders; GNP measures output produced by a country's nationals wherever they are.
items.misconception: GDP and GNP are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: GDP is income and GNP is spending.
items.why: Foreign profits from a UK firm abroad count in UK GNP but not UK GDP. Profits of a Japanese firm in the UK count in UK GDP but Japan's GNP. The distinction matters for globalised economies.

items.q: What is inflation?
items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.q: What is a negative externality?
items.correct: A cost imposed on a third party not involved in the transaction — e.g. pollution from a factory affects neighbours who didn't choose to bear it.
items.misconception: A negative externality is something bad that happens to a business.
items.random_wrong: A negative externality is a tax on a business.
items.why: The 'externality' is external to the market participants — borne by THIRD parties. Students miss the structure: producer, consumer, then someone else who didn't agree to be affected.

items.q: What is the difference between revenue and profit?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity); profit is what remains after deducting costs from revenue.
items.misconception: Revenue and profit mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Revenue is profit before tax and profit is revenue after tax.
items.why: A business with high revenue can still make a loss if costs exceed it. Revenue = sales income; profit = revenue minus costs. Conflating them gives nonsensical analysis.

items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: What defines a pure public good?
items.correct: Non-rivalry (one person's use doesn't reduce others') and non-excludability (you can't stop people benefiting) — e.g. national defence, street lighting.
items.misconception: A public good is anything provided by the government.
items.random_wrong: A public good is anything that's free.
items.why: Government provides many private goods (e.g. NHS treatment is rival). The economic definition is about the GOOD's properties, not who provides it. Students confuse provider with technical category.

items.q: What causes the supply curve to shift?
items.correct: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes/subsidies, number of producers, expectations — anything affecting supply other than the good's own price.
items.misconception: When demand rises, the supply curve shifts right to meet it.
items.random_wrong: Supply shifts only when consumers want more.
items.why: Rising demand causes a movement along the supply curve, not a shift of it. Supply shifts only when something on the supply side changes. Students entangle the two curves.

items.q: What are the functions of money?
items.correct: Medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account, and standard of deferred payment — money must perform these to function as money.
items.misconception: Money is valuable because it is made of valuable materials.
items.random_wrong: Money is only valuable if backed by gold.
items.why: Modern fiat money has no intrinsic value — its value comes from social acceptance and government issuance. Students think paper money 'must' be backed by something; it isn't, and hasn't been since 1971.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: What is structural unemployment?
items.correct: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — often due to long-term economic change like deindustrialisation.
items.misconception: Structural unemployment is unemployment caused by a recession.
items.random_wrong: Structural unemployment is when people are too lazy to work.
items.why: Recession-driven unemployment is CYCLICAL. Structural is about long-term mismatches — coal miners can't easily become app developers. Students collapse all unemployment into 'lack of demand'.

items.q: What did Adam Smith mean by the 'invisible hand'?
items.correct: That individuals pursuing their own self-interest in competitive markets can unintentionally produce socially beneficial outcomes through the price mechanism.
items.misconception: Adam Smith believed all selfish behaviour always benefits society.
items.random_wrong: The invisible hand refers to government regulating the economy secretly.
items.why: Smith was careful — the invisible hand works only under specific conditions (competition, no externalities, etc.). Students reduce him to a pure free-marketeer; his actual position was more nuanced.

items.q: Is a trade deficit necessarily bad?
items.correct: Not necessarily — it can reflect strong domestic demand, attractive investment opportunities, or borrowing to fund growth. Context matters more than the headline figure.
items.misconception: A trade deficit always means a country is losing wealth and failing economically.
items.random_wrong: A trade deficit means a country has no factories.
items.why: The US has run deficits for decades while growing. Trade deficits are balanced by capital inflows. Students treat deficits as automatic failure, missing the accounting identity and what it implies.

items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.

items.q: What does standard theory predict about a minimum wage?
items.correct: If above equilibrium, it raises wages for those still employed but causes unemployment by reducing labour demand — though empirical evidence is mixed.
items.misconception: A minimum wage always reduces unemployment because workers have more money to spend.
items.random_wrong: A minimum wage is paid by the government.
items.why: Students reverse the standard prediction. Empirical evidence is genuinely debated, but the theoretical baseline is that above-equilibrium price floors create surplus (unemployment in labour markets).

items.q: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
items.correct: Countries should specialise in producing goods with lower opportunity cost relative to others — even a country worse at everything still has a comparative advantage in something.
items.misconception: A country should produce whatever it can make best.
items.random_wrong: Comparative advantage means richer countries should help poorer ones.
items.why: Students confuse absolute and comparative advantage. The Ricardian insight is that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country is absolutely better at everything — based on opportunity cost.

items.q: Are monopolies always bad for consumers?
items.correct: Standard theory says monopolies restrict output and raise prices, but possible benefits include economies of scale, R&D investment and dynamic efficiency.
items.misconception: Monopolies are illegal and always harmful.
items.random_wrong: A monopoly is when one country dominates world trade.
items.why: Monopolies aren't automatically illegal — natural monopolies in utilities can be efficient. The analysis requires weighing static inefficiency against potential dynamic gains. Students treat the question as already settled.

items.q: How does raising interest rates reduce inflation?
items.correct: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive, reducing consumption and investment — lowering aggregate demand and slowing price rises.
items.misconception: Higher interest rates directly force shops to lower their prices.
items.random_wrong: Higher interest rates make money more valuable so inflation falls automatically.
items.why: The transmission runs through aggregate demand, not direct price control. Higher rates dampen spending, which cools demand-pull inflation. Students miss the demand-side mechanism.

items.q: What is the difference between nominal and real values?
items.correct: Nominal values are measured in current money; real values are adjusted for inflation to allow meaningful comparison over time.
items.misconception: Nominal means estimated and real means accurate.
items.random_wrong: Nominal values are written and real values are spent.
items.why: Both nominal and real values are 'accurate' — they answer different questions. Real values strip out inflation to reveal actual purchasing power changes. Critical for comparing wages or GDP across years.

items.q: What is the multiplier effect?
items.correct: An initial injection of spending circulates as recipients spend portions, generating more income — total effect on GDP is larger than the initial injection.
items.misconception: The multiplier means government spending is always good because it multiplies wealth.
items.random_wrong: The multiplier is the rate at which interest compounds.
items.why: The multiplier is a mechanism, not a verdict. Whether to use it depends on conditions (slack in economy, marginal propensity to consume). Students confuse the descriptive concept with a policy prescription.

items.q: What is consumer surplus?
items.correct: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay — represented by the area below the demand curve and above the price line.
items.misconception: Consumer surplus is leftover stock that consumers haven't bought yet.
items.random_wrong: Consumer surplus is the profit that consumers make.
items.why: 'Surplus' is misleadingly physical-sounding. It is a welfare measure — the value consumers get beyond what they paid. Students take the everyday meaning of 'surplus' and miss the economic concept.

items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: What does the assumption 'ceteris paribus' mean in economic models?
items.correct: 'All other things equal' — when analysing the effect of one variable, all other variables are held constant. It is an analytical simplification, not a claim about reality.
items.misconception: Ceteris paribus means everything happens at the same time in real markets.
items.random_wrong: Ceteris paribus is a Latin name for the equilibrium price.
items.why: Real-world markets never hold all things equal — ceteris paribus is a thought-experiment device. Students treat it as a description of reality, missing why economists use it: to isolate cause and effect.

items.q: What does 'marginal' mean in economics?
items.correct: The change from one additional unit — marginal cost is the cost of producing one more unit; marginal utility is the satisfaction from one more unit. Marginal is incremental, not total or average.
items.misconception: Marginal cost is the total cost of producing all units.
items.random_wrong: Marginal means unimportant or small.
items.why: 'Marginal' has a specific technical meaning: the next unit. Profit maximisation, optimal consumption and many other results depend on marginal — not total or average — quantities. Students conflate the three measures.

items.q: What does Income Elasticity of Demand (YED) tell us?
items.correct: The sign tells us the good type: positive YED = normal good (rises with income); negative YED = inferior good (falls with income). YED > 1 indicates a luxury; YED between 0 and 1 a necessity.
items.misconception: A negative YED means the demand calculation went wrong.
items.random_wrong: YED measures how the rich and poor differ in tastes.
items.why: The negative sign for inferior goods (like own-brand pasta or bus travel) is meaningful, not an error. Students often treat negative elasticities as mistakes, missing what the sign signals about good type.

items.q: How does the sign of cross elasticity of demand (XED) reveal product relationships?
items.correct: Positive XED = substitutes (price of one rises, demand for the other rises); negative XED = complements (price of one rises, demand for the other falls); zero XED = unrelated goods.
items.misconception: The sign of XED doesn't matter — only the size does.
items.random_wrong: XED measures how often consumers cross-shop between stores.
items.why: The sign carries the qualitative information about the relationship — substitute vs complement. Size tells you the strength of the relationship. Students focus on magnitude and miss the categorical meaning of the sign.

items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: What is a Pigouvian tax?
items.correct: A tax set equal to the external cost of an activity — designed to internalise the externality so the private cost reflects the social cost. Used for pollution, congestion, sugary drinks.
items.misconception: A Pigouvian tax is a tax that funds government spending.
items.random_wrong: A Pigouvian tax is a tax only on rich people.
items.why: Pigouvian taxes are corrective, not revenue-raising. The aim is to align private incentives with social welfare. Students treat all taxes as the same (revenue), missing the distinct purpose of Pigouvian intervention.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.q: What components make up aggregate demand?
items.correct: AD = C + I + G + (X - M): consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports. A shift in any component shifts the AD curve.
items.misconception: Aggregate demand is just the total amount consumers want to buy.
items.random_wrong: Aggregate demand is the same as GDP measured by demand.
items.why: AD covers all spending sources, not just households. Students reduce it to consumer demand, missing that investment, government and trade are equally constitutive. Mis-attributing a shift then misdiagnoses the cause.

items.q: How does quantitative easing actually work?
items.correct: The central bank creates reserves to buy financial assets (mostly government bonds) from banks and pension funds, raising asset prices and lowering yields — stimulating spending and lending indirectly. It is not literally printing physical cash for the public.
items.misconception: QE is the central bank printing money and handing it to citizens.
items.random_wrong: QE is when banks charge negative interest on deposits.
items.why: QE works through asset markets and bank reserves, not direct cash transfers. Students picture printing presses and helicopter drops, missing the indirect channel via bond yields, exchange rates and wealth effects.

items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.

items.q: What are automatic fiscal stabilisers?
items.correct: Features of the tax and benefit system that automatically cushion the economy without new legislation — e.g. unemployment benefits rise in slumps; tax revenues fall as incomes fall, supporting demand.
items.misconception: Automatic stabilisers are interest rate changes by the central bank.
items.random_wrong: Automatic stabilisers are special government emergency funds.
items.why: Stabilisers are fiscal (tax/benefit) and automatic (no decision needed) — distinct from monetary policy or discretionary stimulus. Students confuse them with central bank action or with one-off bailout funds.

items.q: Why do economists generally argue against protectionism?
items.correct: Tariffs and quotas raise prices for consumers, protect inefficient industries, invite retaliation, and reduce overall welfare — even when they save specific jobs, the cost per job saved typically exceeds the wage.
items.misconception: Protectionism always makes a country richer by keeping money at home.
items.random_wrong: Protectionism means protecting national parks from development.
items.why: Trade is positive-sum in standard theory — countries gain from specialisation. Students assume tariffs trap wealth domestically, missing that consumers pay higher prices and exporters lose from retaliation.

items.q: What does dependency theory argue about development?
items.correct: Poorer 'periphery' countries are kept underdeveloped by their economic relationships with rich 'core' countries — through unequal trade, debt and historical colonialism. It challenges the view that integration with rich economies automatically helps.
items.misconception: Dependency theory means poor countries are simply lazy.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is about households relying on benefits.
items.why: Dependency theory is a structural critique of global capitalism, not a behavioural claim about poor countries. Students sometimes invert the analysis entirely or apply 'dependency' to individuals rather than nations.
